key_insight: The paper shows that governing ODEs can be identified from solution data using a Hausdorff-distance view of solution sets, with explicit bounds on when distinct equations are distinguishable and how many observations are needed.
engineering_relevance: This gives scientific ML practitioners a principled way to judge identifiability and data requirements before attempting equation discovery from trajectory measurements.
method: Define a Hausdorff distance between solution sets as the comparison metric for ODEs.
Derive identifiability bounds for linear and nonlinear ODE classes, including Lipschitz/Hölder vector fields.
Use metric entropy to obtain sample-complexity estimates for recovering the governing equation from multiple solution observations.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical analysis rather than experiments, focusing on identifiability, metric entropy, and sample-complexity bounds for ODE classes.
watch_item: The guarantees depend on the chosen ODE class and access to sufficiently rich solution observations, so real-data noise and model mismatch may weaken practical recoverability.
paper_title: Recovering Governing Equations from Solution Data: Identifiability Bounds for Linear and Nonlinear ODEs
